title: Liquid Cooling Without Lock-In: Designing Modular Data Centres for High-Density AI Workloads
text:
Liquid Cooling Without Lock-In: Designing Modular Data Centres for High-Density AI Workloads
The data centre industry is being asked to design for density curves that are still moving.
AI infrastructure is no longer developing in predictable increments. New generations of accelerated compute are changing rack power, thermal loads, cooling topologies and white space planning faster than traditional design cycles can comfortably absorb.
For years, many facilities were designed around relatively stable assumptions: standard rack densities, air-based cooling architectures, defined containment strategies and familiar mechanical distribution models.
That environment has changed.
The International Energy Agency estimates that global electricity consumption from data centres was around 415 TWh in 2024 and projects this to reach around 945 TWh by 2030 in its base case. The same analysis notes that accelerated servers, largely driven by AI adoption, are projected to grow electricity consumption at around 30% annually, compared with slower growth for conventional servers.
That growth is not simply a power problem.
It is a cooling problem. It is a pipework problem. It is an integration problem. It is a design flexibility problem.
For hyperscale operators, the key question is no longer:
“Should we use liquid cooling?”
The more important question is:
“How do we design infrastructure that can support liquid cooling today without locking us into one technology, one vendor, one density profile or one future configuration?”
That is the challenge of liquid cooling without lock-in.
Why High Density Changes the Design Conversation
AI workloads are fundamentally different from traditional enterprise or cloud workloads.
They concentrate compute power into dense clusters. They create extreme thermal loads over smaller physical footprints. They increase demand for low-latency networking, heavier power distribution, advanced controls and highly reliable cooling pathways.
This means that rack density is no longer just an IT specification. It is a first-order design driver for the entire facility.
JLL’s 2025 Global Data Center Outlook stated that across hyperscale and colocation segments, around 10 GW of capacity was projected to break ground globally in 2025, with the global data centre market likely to expand at a baseline 15% CAGR through 2027, potentially reaching 20%. JLL also noted that AI’s power demands are reshaping data centre design and operation.
The result is a market under pressure to deliver more capacity, faster, while designing for technical requirements that continue to evolve.
Historically, air cooling could accommodate most deployments with a combination of CRAH/CRAC units, containment strategies, raised floor or overhead airflow distribution, and efficient heat rejection. Those strategies are still relevant. Air cooling is not disappearing.
But high-density AI environments are moving beyond the point where air alone can be treated as the default long-term answer.
According to the Uptime Institute Cooling Systems Survey 2025, 75% of respondents still used perimeter air cooling, while only 22% reported using direct liquid cooling. However, the same survey found that high rack densities remained the top driver for direct liquid cooling adoption, cited by 69% of respondents in 2025.
This tells us something important.
The industry is not moving from air to liquid overnight. It is moving into a hybrid transition period where air, liquid and mixed strategies need to coexist.
That transition period is where design risk is highest.
Liquid Cooling Is Not One Decision
A common mistake is to treat liquid cooling as a single technology decision.
In reality, liquid cooling is a family of design choices, each with different implications for space, reliability, maintenance, commissioning and future adaptability.
The main pathways include:
Rear-door heat exchangers, which can support higher densities while maintaining a closer relationship to existing rack layouts.
Direct-to-chip liquid cooling, where coolant is delivered to cold plates attached directly to high-power components such as GPUs and CPUs.
Immersion cooling, where IT equipment is submerged in dielectric fluid.
Hybrid air-liquid strategies, where liquid removes the highest heat loads while air continues to manage residual heat and supporting components.
Each approach has different consequences for:
          
      
        
    
This is why the smartest design conversation is not about selecting one cooling method too early.
It is about creating an infrastructure platform that can accommodate multiple pathways.
The Rise of Rack-Scale AI Infrastructure
AI hardware is increasingly being designed at rack scale rather than server scale.
NVIDIA’s GB200 NVL72, for example, connects 36 Grace CPUs and 72 Blackwell GPUs in a rack-scale, liquid-cooled design. NVIDIA describes the system as an exascale computer in a single rack, with the 72 GPUs connected through its NVLink domain.
This is the type of hardware shift that changes data centre design assumptions.
It is not just that individual racks are becoming more powerful. It is that the rack itself is becoming an integrated compute system with specific power, cooling and network requirements.
Vertiv’s co-developed reference architecture for NVIDIA’s GB200 NVL72 platform supports liquid-cooled rack-scale infrastructure of up to 132 kW per rack and is positioned around deployment speed, resiliency, cost, energy efficiency and scalability.
That level of density has major implications for the surrounding infrastructure.
At 100 kW per rack, the white space is no longer just a room full of racks. It becomes a highly coordinated thermal and hydraulic environment.
Power distribution, cooling distribution, structural loading, monitoring, leak detection, redundancy and maintenance access all become interdependent.
The rack is no longer just an endpoint.
It becomes part of the mechanical system.
Pipework Is Becoming a Critical Design Constraint
In traditional air-cooled environments, airflow strategy dominated the cooling conversation. Designers focused on air volume, containment, pressure management, hot aisle/cold aisle layouts and heat rejection efficiency.
In liquid-cooled environments, the new constraint is increasingly pipework.
Pipework affects:
          
      
        
    
This matters because high-density environments have less tolerance for spatial improvisation.
A pipe route that appears manageable in concept design can become a major site issue if it clashes with containment, overhead services, structural steel, cable pathways, fire systems or maintenance zones.
This is why liquid cooling must be designed as a coordinated system, not as an IT-side addition.
If liquid cooling is introduced too late, it forces reactive decisions:
          
      
        
    
The technical issue is not simply whether coolant can reach the rack.
It is whether the system can be installed, tested, maintained and expanded safely over the life of the facility.
The Retrofit Challenge vs The New-Build Challenge
Liquid cooling creates two very different design problems.
The first is retrofit: how do you introduce liquid cooling into facilities originally designed around air?
The second is new build: how do you design a facility today for densities that may rise significantly over the next hardware cycle?
Both are difficult, but they are difficult in different ways.
Retrofit environments
In retrofit projects, the constraints are already fixed.
The structure exists. The white space layout exists. The plant strategy exists. The power architecture exists. The operational procedures exist.
Introducing liquid cooling means working around live operational risk, existing pipe routes, limited access, restricted plant capacity and potentially limited floor loading.
This is why Uptime’s 2025 cooling survey finding is so important: operators ranked ease of retrofit into existing infrastructure as the most important factor in determining direct liquid cooling viability, cited by 46% of respondents.
The industry is not only asking “does liquid cooling work?”
It is asking “can we integrate it without disrupting the facility?”
New-build environments
New builds offer more freedom, but also more uncertainty.
The challenge is not only designing for today’s equipment. It is designing for future AI hardware that may have different rack densities, coolant requirements, connection details and maintenance needs.
Overcommitting to one pathway too early can create lock-in.
Under-designing the infrastructure can create expensive retrofit requirements later.
The balance is to create defined flexibility: enough capacity, space and interface logic to support future change, without overbuilding every element of the facility.
That is where modular design becomes strategically important.
The Real Barriers to Liquid Cooling Adoption
Liquid cooling is technically mature enough to be deployed, but adoption is still constrained by practical concerns.
The Uptime Institute Cooling Systems Survey 2025 identified lack of standardisation across systems as the top barrier to direct liquid cooling adoption, cited by 39% of respondents. Other major barriers included cost at 38%, reliability concerns at 35%, limited equipment/vendor choice at 26%, and maintenance issues at 26%.
These barriers are not abstract.
They map directly onto real project risks:
Lack of standardisation creates interface uncertainty between IT hardware, CDUs, manifolds, pipework, controls and facility systems.
Cost concerns increase when systems are designed late, redesigned repeatedly or procured as bespoke packages.
Reliability concerns increase when failure modes, isolation strategies and leak detection are not integrated from the beginning.
Maintenance concerns increase when access, replacement procedures and operational training are treated as afterthoughts.
This is why the transition to liquid cooling is not just a cooling technology transition.
It is a delivery model transition.
Designing for Liquid Cooling Without Lock-In
A future-ready liquid cooling strategy should avoid two extremes.
The first extreme is rigid commitment: designing the facility around a single cooling technology or vendor architecture before future IT requirements are fully known.
The second extreme is vague future-proofing: adding space “just in case” without defined interface standards, pipework routes, commissioning logic or expansion rules.
Both approaches carry risk.
The better approach is modular adaptability.
This means defining a platform where the key interfaces are standardised, while the final cooling configuration can evolve.
A liquid-cooling-ready modular platform should consider:
1. Defined cooling distribution zones
High-density AI clusters rarely spread evenly across the white space. They often concentrate in specific zones. That means the cooling architecture should allow for zonal deployment rather than facility-wide disruption.
Cooling distribution modules, CDU zones and pipework corridors should be planned around expansion logic.
The question is not only “where does the first liquid-cooled cluster go?”
It is “where does the second, third and fourth cluster go?”
2. Modular CDU and pump integration
Coolant Distribution Units are becoming a key interface between IT systems and facility cooling infrastructure.
Their location affects pressure drop, maintenance access, redundancy, leak containment and future expansion.
A modular approach allows CDU and pump assemblies to be factory-built, pre-tested and deployed in repeatable configurations.
This supports:
          
      
        
    
Factory-built modules also reduce the amount of complex pipework and control integration required in live or congested site conditions.
3. Standardised pipework interfaces
Pipework will increasingly determine whether liquid cooling is easy to deploy or difficult to manage.
Standardisation should cover:
          
      
        
    
Without this discipline, each liquid cooling deployment becomes a bespoke integration exercise.
With it, liquid cooling becomes a repeatable infrastructure layer.
4. Hybrid cooling pathways
The transition to liquid cooling will not be uniform.
Some deployments will require direct-to-chip cooling. Others may remain air-cooled with higher-efficiency containment. Some will use rear-door heat exchangers as a bridge strategy. Many will use hybrid configurations.
A modular MEP platform should therefore allow air and liquid systems to coexist.
This includes:
          
      
        
    
The design goal should not be “liquid everywhere immediately.”
It should be “liquid where required, air where effective, and infrastructure that can change when the load profile changes.”
5. Commissioning designed from day one
Liquid cooling changes commissioning.
It introduces additional requirements around:
          
      
        
    
These cannot be treated as late-stage activities.
They need to be embedded into the design, manufacturing and installation strategy.
The value of off-site manufacturing is significant here. Factory Acceptance Testing can validate assemblies before they reach site, reducing commissioning uncertainty and helping site teams focus on integration rather than discovery.
Why Modular Construction Matters More in Liquid-Cooled Environments
Liquid cooling increases the value of modular construction because it increases the importance of precision.
In air-cooled environments, many system elements can still be adapted during installation. In liquid-cooled environments, tolerances, routes, access points and interfaces are less forgiving.
Factory-built modular systems allow critical elements to be manufactured under controlled conditions, including:
          
      
        
    
The benefits are not only speed-related.
They are quality-related.
They are safety-related.
They are commissioning-related.
They are operational-readiness-related.
This aligns closely with the broader direction of modular delivery: moving risk away from congested sites and into controlled environments where repeatability, testing and documentation can be managed more effectively.
The White Space Is No Longer Just White Space
As liquid cooling becomes more common, the boundary between white space and grey space becomes less simple.
Mechanical infrastructure is moving closer to the rack.
Pipework, manifolds, CDUs, sensors and leak detection systems are becoming part of the white space planning conversation.
That changes the relationship between IT, MEP, architecture and operations.
Design teams must now coordinate:
          
      
        
    
The risk is that each discipline optimises its own area without fully resolving the interfaces.
In high-density environments, interface failure is often where programme risk appears.
The cooling system may work. The rack may work. The power system may work. But the integrated environment may still be difficult to build, operate or expand.
This is why early coordination matters.
The Case for Platform Thinking
The industry does not need a different bespoke cooling design for every AI deployment.
It needs adaptable platforms.
A platform approach allows operators to define a repeatable base system with controlled flexibility for future change.
That platform should include:
          
      
        
    
This is the same logic that underpins scalable modular construction more broadly.
Design once. Validate thoroughly. Manufacture repeatedly. Adapt intelligently.
The value is not that every site becomes identical.
The value is that every site starts from a proven, coordinated system.
Designing for What We Do Not Yet Know
Perhaps the biggest challenge in liquid cooling is uncertainty.
Rack densities are still evolving. AI hardware cycles are accelerating. Cooling technologies are competing and converging. Facility owners are being asked to make long-life infrastructure decisions around fast-moving IT requirements.
That is why the goal should not be to predict the exact future.
The goal should be to design for structured uncertainty.
This means creating infrastructure with:
          
      
        
    
The facilities that succeed will not simply be the ones that choose liquid cooling early.
They will be the ones that integrate it intelligently.
Looking Ahead
Liquid cooling is not a trend sitting somewhere on the horizon.
It is already reshaping high-density data centre design.
But the real challenge is not adoption. It is integration.
The next generation of data centres will need to support higher rack densities, faster hardware refresh cycles, more complex cooling topologies and greater pressure to deliver at speed.
That requires a new design philosophy.
One that treats cooling not as a standalone system, but as part of an adaptable infrastructure platform.
One that recognises pipework, access, commissioning and maintainability as strategic design issues.
One that avoids lock-in while still creating enough certainty to manufacture, install and operate with confidence.
At Modulise, this is where modular thinking becomes most valuable: translating high-density requirements into coordinated, factory-built, installation-ready systems that reduce site complexity and support future adaptability.
As AI continues to redefine data centre infrastructure, the winners will not simply be those who cool the highest-density racks.
They will be the ones who design systems that can keep evolving.
